raw_data:
b' Matt Levine's Money Stuff
Programming note: Money Stuff will be off tomorrow, back on Monday.The stereotype of ChatGPT, Open AI\xe2\x80\x99s publicly available conversational la

Programming note: Money Stuff will be off tomorrow, back on Monday.

ChatGPT

The stereotype of ChatGPT , Open AI\xe2\x80\x99s publicly available conversational large language model, is that if you ask it a technical question it will give you an answer that is confident and plausible but not necessarily correct. This is of course an incredibly useful skill, and a very good model of a certain sort of human intelligence; ChatGPT seems dangerous as a software engineer but could have a good run as an investment banker. [1]

How is it as an asset manager, or at least an asset-management salesrobot? Bloomberg\xe2\x80\x99s Sam Potter and Katherine Greifeld gave it a go :

In a bid to see how close technology really is to replacing Wall Street\xe2\x80\x99s army of analysts, experts and money runners, we challenged ChatGPT, the artificial intelligence tool that\xe2\x80\x99s taking the internet by storm, to create us a winning portfolio for the US stock market.

The result: A classic exercise in fence-sitting, with the tool explaining that the market is too unpredictable to design such a fund, while warning about the need to pick investments aligning with our goals and appetite for risk-taking.

\xe2\x80\x9cIt is not possible for me to design an ETF that will beat the US stock market because the stock market is unpredictable and past performance does not guarantee future results,\xe2\x80\x9d ChatGPT says, disappointingly, adding a bunch of not-investing-advice boilerplate. Bleh. I would have expected more unearned confidence. Like I\xe2\x80\x99d expect it to come up with a list of 50 tickers, no problem; I just wouldn\xe2\x80\x99t expect them to beat the market, and maybe a couple of them would not correspond to actual stocks.

You can do better, where by \xe2\x80\x9cbetter\xe2\x80\x9d I mean \xe2\x80\x9cget more confident answers out of ChatGPT\xe2\x80\x9d rather than \xe2\x80\x9cactually get it to beat the market for you.\xe2\x80\x9d Last month Robin Wigglesworth at the Financial Times wrote about a quantitative research analyst who got ChatGPT to write research notes for him; they were, you know, adequate.\xc2\xa0And I once wrote about GPT-2, an earlier large language model:

If you trained it on a bunch of Warren Buffett annual letters maybe it would say some stuff that sounds like Warren Buffett? Not just in terms of folksy sex jokes but also in terms of penetrating investment insight? Maybe GPT-2 would digest Buffett\xe2\x80\x99s mind, or rather specifically the parts of Buffett\xe2\x80\x99s mind that are exposed when he writes prose, and it would use that understanding of his mind to write Buffett-like prose recommending Buffett-like investing decisions?

Or if you run an investment firm and you\xe2\x80\x99ve got a corpus of memos from your analysts recommending investment decisions, why not take the memos that worked out \xe2\x80\x94the ones recommending investments that went up\xe2\x80\x94and feed them into GPT-2? Then have it write you a new memo and see if it\xe2\x80\x99s any good?

I wouldn\xe2\x80\x99t bet on it or anything; it\xe2\x80\x99s interesting to speculate about, but it seems unlikely that a computer will get good at making investment decisions just by ingesting how humans have articulated investment\xc2\xa0decisions. (It\xe2\x80\x99s much more likely to fixate on boilerplate about how \xe2\x80\x9cpast performance does not guarantee future results.\xe2\x80\x9d) But I bet a computer could get pretty good at articulating investment decisions from that training set. (In a sense this is the opposite of a classic \xe2\x80\x9cblack box\xe2\x80\x9d machine-learning investment algorithm: Classic quant signals might work, but you can\xe2\x80\x99t explain them ; ChatGPT might not work, but it can explain itself.)\xc2\xa0If your hedge fund lost money last year, maybe ask ChatGPT to write your investor letter for you. It can spin up some platitudes, quote some Stoic philosophers, and sound confident and plausible, which is all you really need for the letter .

Twitter interest payment

Tomorrow seems to be the due date for Twitter Inc.\xe2\x80\x99s first interest payment on the $13 billion it borrowed to fund Elon Musk\xe2\x80\x99s buyout. That\xe2\x80\x99s a little exciting!\xc2\xa0Will Twitter pay? I mean, probably. Here is a Bloomberg News article from last week being like \xe2\x80\x9coh come on, obviously Elon is gonna pay, Twitter has the money and it\xe2\x80\x99d be crazy not to\xe2\x80\x9d:

By all accounts \xe2\x80\x94 including Elon Musk\xe2\x80\x99s \xe2\x80\x94 Twitter has more than enough money to make its first interest payments, expected to total about $300 million. \xe2\x80\xa6

A group of seven banks, led by Morgan Stanley, own the debt. \xe2\x80\xa6 Now, after they lost some $4 billion on paper for backing Musk\xe2\x80\x99s Twitter bid, market onlookers see little reason for the banks to go along with any unexpected maneuvers near the interest-payment deadline, roughly around Jan. 27.

After all, in most bankruptcies, the equity is wiped out \xe2\x80\x94 and lenders eventually take control.

\xe2\x80\x9cThere\xe2\x80\x99s too much at stake for Musk and his co-investors,\xe2\x80\x9d said Jordan Chalfin, a senior analyst at credit research firm CreditSights. \xe2\x80\x9cTwitter will make its near-term interest payments, come hell or high water, and give the business time to turn around.\xe2\x80\x9d

On the other hand \xe2\x80\xa6\xc2\xa0it\xe2\x80\x99s Elon Musk? And some banks? And Twitter? Like:

  • Musk does not care about doing the normal, expected, pro-social thing, or frankly even the thing that is convenient for him. \xe2\x80\x9cYou are supposed to pay the interest, and if you don\xe2\x80\x99t it will be very unfair to your banks and create a lot of drama and distraction and litigation for you\xe2\x80\x9d: Just not a set of arguments that will work on him, you know? Twitter isn\xe2\x80\x99t paying rent on its offices . Why pay interest on its loans?
  • If he doesn\xe2\x80\x99t pay, what are the banks gonna do? Declare a default? Accelerate the debt? Throw Twitter into bankruptcy? \xe2\x80\x9cAfter all, in most bankruptcies, the equity is wiped out \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0and lenders eventually take control.\xe2\x80\x9d Of Twitter? Does Morgan Stanley want to own Twitter, particularly after Elon Musk is through petulantly trashing it? It absolutely does not! \xe2\x80\x9cNo, we could not end up owning Twitter,\xe2\x80\x9d Morgan Stanley Chief Executive Officer James Gorman said on Bloomberg Television last week, chuckling at the absurdity of the suggestion. That is completely correct, but not something you want to admit to Elon Musk. Now you have no leverage with him!\xc2\xa0He knows that if he defaults, you won\xe2\x80\x99t seize the company. If Twitter defaults on its interest payment, its banks will send it a series of increasingly frantic and threatening emails, but the threats will be hollow. Waiting for Twitter\xe2\x80\x99s financial position to improve, and then asking Musk politely for the overdue\xc2\xa0interest when he\xe2\x80\x99s in a better mood, might actually be more sensible for the banks than playing hardball now. And Musk knows that!
  • There remains the possibility that Musk could buy some of the debt from the banks. The banks want to sell it \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0they are not normally in the business of holding on to buyout debt for this long \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0and if Musk bought it he could lower his interest payments . [2] The debt does not trade, but there have been reports of banks getting bids for some of it at 60 cents on the dollar . The worse the debt gets \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0for instance if Twitter doesn\xe2\x80\x99t pay interest \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0the lower that price will go. If it gets to, like, 30, maybe Musk will want to buy and the banks will be desperate to sell. In theory, the more unpleasant he makes it for the banks, the cheaper the debt will get.

To be clear I still assume that Twitter will make its interest payment on time, mostly because not paying the interest would require a lot of time and attention from Musk and my guess is that he has higher-priority hijinks to attend to. But also because Twitter and Musk\xe2\x80\x99s other businesses keep needing financial services and going to war with the banks over this will cause problems. But I\xe2\x80\x99m not certain. Just because Twitter can make the payment, and should , doesn\xe2\x80\x99t mean that it will.

NYSE

We talked yesterday about a glitch at the New York Stock Exchange on Tuesday: NYSE forgot to do the opening auction for a bunch of stocks, instead going straight into ordinary trading at 9:30 a.m. It turns out this is bad: If you take the orders for the opening auction and just put them into the regular trading order book, prices bounce around wildly and you end up having to cancel a bunch of trades. The stock market works one way in the opening auction at 9:30:00, and a different way in regular trading at 9:30:01, and if you mix them up you get a mess.

But how did NYSE mix them up? Bloomberg\xe2\x80\x99s Katherine Doherty reports :

More than 700 miles from Wall Street, the New York Stock Exchange\xe2\x80\x99s backup data center on Cermak Road in Chicago is supposed to safeguard US markets, standing by at all hours in case disaster ever strikes the world\xe2\x80\x99s largest venue for trading shares.

When markets are closed, it participates in a well-worn routine, with NYSE staffers turning on and off systems to ensure everything works. But heading into Tuesday, an NYSE employee failed to properly shut down Cermak\xe2\x80\x99s disaster-recovery system \xe2\x80\x94 leading to a disaster. ...

The turmoil resulted because the backup system in Cermak was left running. That misled the exchange\xe2\x80\x99s computers to treat the 9:30 a.m. opening bell as a continuation of trading, and so they skipped the day\xe2\x80\x99s opening auctions that neatly set initial prices.

Eep. I wrote yesterday that \xe2\x80\x9cthe stock market goes to bed at 4 p.m. and wakes up at 9:30 a.m. the next day.\xe2\x80\x9d But in fact, when NYSE\xc2\xa0goes to bed at 4 p.m.,\xc2\xa0it spends the night dreaming that it is still processing trades. And then it wakes up at 9:30 a.m. the next day to process more trades. On Tuesday, it kept dreaming through its 9:30 alarm clock, and chaos resulted.

Incidentally! The US Securities and Exchange Commission has proposed new rules that would significantly change how retail stock trading works. Oversimplifying enormously, the way it works now is that retail brokers send their customers\xe2\x80\x99 orders to electronic market making firms, who trade directly with those orders, buying from the customers who want to sell and selling to the ones who want to buy and paying the brokers a bit for the privilege. And the SEC\xe2\x80\x99s proposal would require the brokers to instead send those orders to a stock exchange , like NYSE or Nasdaq, which would then run an auction to decide who trades with the retail order. [3] We discussed the proposals in more detail last month; my headline was \xe2\x80\x9c The SEC Wants More Stock Auctions .\xe2\x80\x9d

The fact that NYSE messed up a bunch of stock auctions this week, and mild chaos resulted, seems mildly bad for that plan? One thing about US market structure is that there are a dozen or so stock exchanges, and you can trade all the stocks on all of them, and if one of them shuts down at 11 a.m. due to a blown fuse or whatever, you just trade at another exchange and everything works fine. But there are only two main listing exchanges \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0NYSE and Nasdaq \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0and stocks are normally listed at one or the other, and the opening and closing auction for each stock happens only on its listing exchange. Yesterday, when NYSE forgot to do its opening auction, that caused chaos everywhere; trading on other exchanges could not compensate for the problem with NYSE\xe2\x80\x99s auctions.

I guess you might worry that if NYSE did hundreds of auctions per day in each stock, instead of two, there would be more problems. If you were a retail broker or electronic market maker, and you like the current system and would be inconvenienced by the SEC\xe2\x80\x99s proposals, you might argue that, anyway. Doherty reports:

Charles Schwab Corp., the brokerage catering to retail investors, openly criticized the NYSE on Wednesday, saying it\xe2\x80\x99s disappointed by how the incident was handled and that individuals may not get a fair shake when it comes to compensation. Schwab said the episode should also raise concerns about regulatory proposals that could push more trading onto stock exchanges.

\xe2\x80\x9cUnfortunately, the NYSE has not owned up to their full responsibility and retail investors will have to go through a lengthy process to correct orders, with no guarantee of a reasonable outcome,\xe2\x80\x9d Schwab spokesperson Mayura Hooper said. \xe2\x80\x9cIt further heightens our concerns that routing even greater levels of retail orders to the exchanges will dramatically reduce the quality of the investing experience for America\xe2\x80\x99s retail investors.\xe2\x80\x9d

NYSE picked sort of an awkward time to mess up its auctions, is the point here.

Tokenomics: DeWi

Here is a Substack post by Connor Lovely with the unpromising title \xe2\x80\x9cDeWi\xe2\x80\x99s first rug pull?\xe2\x80\x9d \xe2\x80\x9cDeWi (decentralized wireless) may be experiencing its first rug pull, vampire attack, and miner strike all at once,\xe2\x80\x9d ah.\xc2\xa0It\xe2\x80\x99s about a crypto project called Pollen Mobile, which \xe2\x80\x9claunched in February 2022 and quickly became Helium\xe2\x80\x99s most formidable peer in the DeWi space.\xe2\x80\x9d We have talked about Helium before , and I am not sure that \xe2\x80\x9cHelium\xe2\x80\x99s most formidable peer\xe2\x80\x9d is something I\xe2\x80\x99d want to put on my resume, but whatever. The basic idea in \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0I\xe2\x80\x99m so sorry \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0DeWi is:

  • Someone starts a project to provide wireless service everywhere, but with crypto.
  • The way it works is that you can get some sort of hardware device from the project, and you plug it in to your home electricity and internet, it provides a wireless\xc2\xa0signal, and people who are near you can connect to the internet using your device.
  • And then \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0this is the crypto part \xe2\x80\x94 they pay for using the coverage you provide, and you get paid for that coverage, in the project\xe2\x80\x99s crypto tokens.

Why don\xe2\x80\x99t they just pay in dollars? Why don\xe2\x80\x99t you just get paid in dollars? Well! Because, as I have said a few times before , the basic premise of a lot of crypto is that every product is simultaneously an investment opportunity. The day that this project \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0Pollen, Helium, whatever \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0launches, (1) nobody is providing service on its network and (2) nobody is using its network. The project is only good if it achieves scale and network effects: You\xe2\x80\x99ll only provide service if people use the network, and you\xe2\x80\x99ll only use the network if people provide service. It\xe2\x80\x99s hard to incentivize people to start .

Crypto \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0or, more specifically, the crypto boom that\xc2\xa0sort of ended last spring \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0was a way to solve this problem. Instead of \xe2\x80\x9cbuy our device and start providing wireless coverage, and if a lot of people do that then people will use the coverage, and then maybe one day they\xe2\x80\x99ll pay you,\xe2\x80\x9d it was \xe2\x80\x9cbuy our device and start providing wireless coverage, and we\xe2\x80\x99ll give you lots of tokens , and if this thing takes off then the tokens will be valuable and you\xe2\x80\x99ll be able to buy an island, for providing this wireless coverage in the early days of our network.\xe2\x80\x9d It was a way to turn this possibly useful service into also a Ponzi scheme: You get rich if more people come into the project after you. But if more people come into the project after you, then it will actually provide a useful service (wireless coverage), and maybe the economics will work out. It\xe2\x80\x99s a little bit of Ponzi to bootstrap a real business with network effects.

One problem is that if this works and you get a lot of people to get your device and plug it in and provide the wireless coverage, the next step is to get people to use it\xc2\xa0and pay for it. And so you have to go to a whole new set of people and be like \xe2\x80\x9chi, would you like to fly a drone in rural areas? Good news, you can use our wireless service!\xe2\x80\x9d And they are like \xe2\x80\x9chmm I\xe2\x80\x99m interested,\xe2\x80\x9d and you continue: \xe2\x80\x9cGreat! You\xe2\x80\x99ll need our tokens to pay for the service. What you do is you set up a MetaMask wallet and buy some Ether, and then you go to a decentralized exchange to swap it for\xe2\x80\x9d but they have already wandered away.\xc2\xa0Come on! You can find some hobbyists at the overlap between \xe2\x80\x9cwant to set up a public WiFi device in their home\xe2\x80\x9d and \xe2\x80\x9care into crypto,\xe2\x80\x9d or at least you could in February 2022, but you will have a harder time finding a lot of people who want to get deep into crypto just to get internet access.

So a natural thing to do is, as\xc2\xa0your network grows, you say \xe2\x80\x9cokay fine you can just pay for our service using a credit card like a normal person.\xe2\x80\x9d But then \xe2\x80\xa6\xc2\xa0what are the tokens for? Nothing, the answer is nothing. You used a Ponzi scheme to build a real service, and then you ran the real service like a regular business. What happens to the token holders?

Anyway Pollen put up a Medium post this week saying that \xe2\x80\x9cIn conjunction with officially announcing Paid Data, we\xe2\x80\x99re also introducing the US dollar as i) the primary means to purchase data and / or plans from Pollen, and ii) the means in which Flower owners will be paid for hauling data.\xe2\x80\x9d And then another one saying :

Pollen\xe2\x80\x99s mission has always been focused on building a real network delivering real utility. Over the past year, we\xe2\x80\x99ve been focused on achieving that goal, delivering a number of industry firsts.

In doing so, we\xe2\x80\x99ve also learned a lot of industry firsts, including two critical, hard truths.

  1. A wireless network that doesn\xe2\x80\x99t have a clear path to profitability via paid data is not a viable network
  2. A token-only model is not a path to a commercially viable mobile network, particularly in the current regulatory environment

After coming to these conclusions, we determined the best path forward towards building a viable network \xe2\x80\x94 including the introduction of US dollar data payments \xe2\x80\x94 and communicated that to the community as soon as possible.

Those two \xe2\x80\x9chard truths\xe2\x80\x9d strike me as completely, obviously correct, [4] but people are understandably mad about this. Lovely writes :

Upon first glance, the post was bullish. \xe2\x80\xa6\xc2\xa0Even the introduction of the good old U.S. dollar as a means to pay for data transfer seemed bullish and sensible. After all, which boomer TradWi (traditional wireless) corporation do you know of that is willing to buy, hold, and sell an illiquid crypto token that trades on one janky DEX to pay for data transfer on a fledgling CBRS network?

Things took a definitive turn for the worse as readers realized that there would be no buy (let alone buy and burn) mechanism to accrue the value of this dollar-denominated data demand to the network\xe2\x80\x99s native token that all the earliest builders of the network had been compensated in! The entire ethos of a decentralized, crypto-powered, community-built wireless network had been thrown out the window.

Fast-forwarding to the present, it\xe2\x80\x99s safe to say that the community is outraged, accusations of a rug pull abound, and many in DeWi are very disillusioned and disappointed by this decision.

Well, yes! One vision of DeWi, sorry, is that everything will be crypto: The service will be so useful and essential that everyone will have no choice but to use its tokens, and crypto exchanges will respond to the demand by becoming easier for regular people to use, and regulators will respond to the public clamor for DeWi tokens by saying \xe2\x80\x9csure whatever sell those tokens, they\xe2\x80\x99re not securities, you don\xe2\x80\x99t need to register them.\xe2\x80\x9d And in February 2022 that was not such a crazy thing to think, and building this in crypto provided the \xe2\x80\x9ctokenomics\xe2\x80\x9d that made people want to set up these wireless devices and join the network and do the whole thing.

But in January 2023 it is clear that the crypto part is a drawback: If you build an excellent widespread wireless network and go out to consumers and corporate customers and say \xe2\x80\x9chey we have a great network, you should use it, just buy our crypto token,\xe2\x80\x9d they will say \xe2\x80\x9chmm sounds good except for the token.\xe2\x80\x9d So you get rid of the token, and all the tokenomics fall apart, and the people who bought in because they thought they\xe2\x80\x99d get rich off the token feel cheated.

Also another, related point is that as far as I can tell every decentralized crypto project has some central group of decision-makers who actually run the project,\xc2\xa0and then a veneer of decentralization. Shouldn\xe2\x80\x99t the holders of Pollen tokens get to decide on its monetization strategy? Lol, no, absolutely not. Sure if they were shareholders of Pollen they\xe2\x80\x99d have some legally binding governance rights. But they\xe2\x80\x99re not.

Tokenomics: Bankruptcy

Oh sure, sure :

Celsius Network LLC is considering issuing a new digital-asset token to repay creditors as part of a proposal to reorganize and exit bankruptcy as a regulated crypto platform, the company said in court Tuesday.

Reorganizing Celsius into a publicly-traded company that is properly licensed would bring in more money for creditors than selling hard-to-liquidate assets at today\xe2\x80\x99s depressed prices, company attorney Ross M. Kwasteniet said during a video-court hearing.

Celsius has been negotiating with various creditor groups over how to set up the new company and issue a new token to creditors as part of a payout plan, Kwasteniet told US Bankruptcy Judge Martin Glenn, who is in New York.

Another troubled crypto platform had created digital assets to cover customer losses, but Celsius is likely the first crypto company to try to issue a new token \xe2\x80\x94 which must be blessed by a federal judge \xe2\x80\x94 to help buy its way out of bankruptcy.

A group of creditors has been pushing Celsius to follow the example set by the Bitfinex exchange, which issued new tokens to customers who lost money due to a hack. In September, a Beijing-based provider of Bitcoin mining-pool services said it would issue tokens to clients equal to the value of various crypto assets that had been frozen.

I mean. What you do is, you restart Celsius as a crypto lending project. You give everyone who lost money new, I dunno, Kelvin tokens that represent their Celsius bankruptcy claims, and you let those Kelvin tokens trade, and the newly restarted Celsius runs a lending market in which retail customers can deposit their Kelvin tokens and earn 18% interest and Celsius will lend them to crypto hedge funds that will steal them. And then new Celsius will go extra bankrupt, and the people whose Kelvin tokens were stolen will get yet another Extra Bankruptcy Token, and you can just repeat this forever. At some level, who cares? Somebody made up the crypto tokens that Celsius lost in the first place; what does it matter if they are replaced with new tokens that Celsius made up? The point is to make sure that everyone has a good time trading and gets paid 18% interest (paid in kind!) on their made-up money until it disappears and is replaced with a new made-up money.

Somebody is reading Money Stuff

A reader\xc2\xa0emailed yesterday to ask:

If Congress ever bans stock buybacks, could a responsible CFO do some token security fraud and settle at an unreasonably large value to return money to shareholders in a tax efficient way?

I am not going to explain this!\xc2\xa0It seems a little extreme, but also I love it? I\xe2\x80\x99m not sure that it\xe2\x80\x99s actually more tax-efficient than paying a dividend, [5] and in any case surely paying a dividend is overall more efficient than this plan, because the securities-fraud angle involves paying a large cut to lawyers and also to former shareholders. But if Congress ever bans stock buybacks,\xc2\xa0some investment bank had better model up this idea.

Somebody else is\xc2\xa0reading Money Stuff

Here is a speech that Hester Peirce, a commissioner on the US Securities and Exchange Commission, gave at a crypto conference last week, in which she was critical of the SEC\xe2\x80\x99s approach to crypto regulation:

The SEC needs to conduct better, more precise, and more transparent legal analysis. The SEC\xe2\x80\x99s approach to regulating crypto looks somewhat like a decidedly unromantic version of CompuDate from the old TV show: we tell people to come down to the office to talk to us about their projects, plug the information they give us into our proprietary security-identifying algorithms, and then send the people home with a court date. Hardly a reasonable way forward, and one that results in what one lawyer has dubbed \xe2\x80\x9cregulation by anxiety.\xe2\x80\x9d Operating in such an opaque environment is very stressful for law-abiding people.

Honestly it\xe2\x80\x99s not that opaque: If you go to the SEC and ask for permission to do a crypto thing, they will say no. Totally clear.

Anyway Peirce also criticizes the SEC for pursuing too much crypto fraud , because sometimes that fraud happens outside of the US and in crypto tokens that are not securities . \xe2\x80\x9cAlthough some might suggest otherwise,\xe2\x80\x9d she says, \xe2\x80\x9ceverything, everywhere is not securities fraud.\xe2\x80\x9d A footnote cites me . I am the one who suggests otherwise .

Here I want to point out that, while Peirce is an SEC commissioner, she is a Republican commissioner who finds herself in the minority on a lot of SEC decisions, and this is a speech in which she is disagreeing with the actual actions and legal theories of the SEC. This speech does not represent the official position of the SEC; it is almost the opposite.\xc2\xa0I think you should read her comment to mean \xe2\x80\x9cregrettably, it is the official position of the SEC that everything, everywhere is securities fraud.\xe2\x80\x9d Because it is.

Things happen

Morgan Stanley Fines Its Bankers Over Messaging Breaches. Stripe Plans to Go Public in Next Year or Make a Deal to Resolve Employee Stock Squeeze . Ghana Payment Halt Is Potential Repudiation, Swap Panel Rules. Zimbabwe Plans a New City for the Rich . Blackstone Misses Forecast for Running $1 Trillion by End of 2022. Rogue AI \xe2\x80\x98 could kill everyone ,\xe2\x80\x99 scientists warn as ChatGPT craze runs rampant. \xe2\x80\x9cNot only did she include a Martin Luther King Jr. quote in a companywide email about layoffs, she girlboss-ified it by removing the word \xe2\x80\x98man\xe2\x80\x99.\xe2\x80\x9d Six doctors swallowed Lego heads for science .

If you'd like to get\xc2\xa0Money\xc2\xa0Stuff\xc2\xa0in handy email form, right in your inbox, please subscribe at this link . Or you can subscribe to Money Stuff and other great Bloomberg newsletters here . Thanks!

[1] Dan Davies last month : \xe2\x80\x9cIt\xe2\x80\x99s plausible and articulate, and people tend to get quite fond of it, even though it has a nasty habit of making things up when it doesn\xe2\x80\x99t know the answer \xe2\x80\x94 the GPT-3 Chatbot has all the qualities necessary to be a successful summer intern.\xe2\x80\x9d

[2] Strictly speaking, if *Twitter* bought the debt and retired it, it could lower its interest payments; if *Musk* bought it then Twitter could make the interest payments *to him*. Or he could cancel the debt that he bought, either out of the goodness of his heart or in exchange for more equity, and lower the interest bill. Musk and Twitter are related parties, but not quite the same: Musk is not legally on the hook for Twitter\xe2\x80\x99s payment obligations, and Twitter has some minority shareholders who came into the deal with Musk.

[3] Technically the proposed rule would require open auctions on some sort of open-access venue, but would not mandate that that venue be one of the national securities exchanges like NYSE or Nasdaq. In practice, though, the requirements do seem to limit the auction venues to the big stock exchanges.

[4] Particularly \xe2\x80\x9cparticularly in the current regulatory environment.\xe2\x80\x9d The US Securities and Exchange Commission, in 2023, is just not going to let you distribute some DeWi token to retail users; the SEC is going to say that\xe2\x80\x99s a security and that you\xe2\x80\x99re doing an unregistered offering.

[5] Hoo boy is this not tax or legal advice, but wouldn't the securities fraud class action settlement be taxable income? Whereas buybacks are famously more tax-efficient than dividends because in a buyback (1) selling shareholders get some income but also some non-taxable return of basis and (2) *non*-selling shareholders don\xe2\x80\x99t get taxed, but see the value of their shares go up.
